Hennig_2006_itraconazole

Source: vignettes/articles/Hennig_2006_itraconazole.Rmd
Hennig_2006_itraconazole.Rmd

Model and source 

#> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'
  • Citation: Hennig S, Wainwright CE, Bell SC, Miller H, Friberg LE, Charles BG. Population pharmacokinetics of itraconazole and its active metabolite hydroxy-itraconazole in paediatric cystic fibrosis and bone marrow transplant patients. Clin Pharmacokinet. 2006;45(11):1099-1114. doi:10.2165/00003088-200645110-00004
  • Description: Population PK model for oral itraconazole and its active metabolite hydroxy-itraconazole in paediatric cystic-fibrosis and bone-marrow-transplant patients (Hennig 2006). One-compartment parent + one-compartment metabolite with first-order absorption, first-order metabolic conversion (fm fixed to 1), allometric weight scaling on parent CL/F (0.75) and Vd/F (1.0), and formulation-specific ka and relative bioavailability for capsule vs oral solution.
  • Article: https://doi.org/10.2165/00003088-200645110-00004

The packaged model implements the Hennig 2006 final model: a one-compartment parent (itraconazole) connected to a one-compartment active metabolite (hydroxy-itraconazole) via a first-order conversion rate kpm = cl / vc with the fraction metabolised fm fixed to 1 (the paper notes that fm could not be estimated because no IV reference was available, so the typical-value model treats every disappearing parent molecule as a metabolite molecule). Allometric scaling on parent CL/F (exponent 0.75) and Vd/F (exponent 1.0) standardises to 70 kg per the paper’s Methods (Eq. 5). Capsule and oral-solution arms are distinguished by a binary FORM_CAPSULE covariate that switches the absorption rate constant between 0.96 h^-1 (oral solution) and 0.09 h^-1 (capsule), and that gates the relative-bioavailability term so the capsule arm carries a typical-value Frel = 0.55 with IIV etalfdepot while the oral-solution arm is fixed at Frel = 1 with no IIV.

Population

Hennig 2006 Table I summarises 49 patients enrolled at The Royal Children’s Hospital and The Prince Charles Hospital (Brisbane, Australia) between April 2003 and April 2005: 29 cystic-fibrosis (CF) patients (24 paediatric and 5 adult) prescribed itraconazole for allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA), and 20 paediatric bone-marrow-transplant (BMT) patients prescribed itraconazole for fungal-infection prophylaxis. Median age was 8 years (range 0.4-30 years; 5 adult CF patients aged 19-30 years were pooled with the 44 paediatric patients for comparison), median total bodyweight 29.3 kg (range 6.8-83.5 kg), median lean bodyweight 25.5 kg, median height 132.5 cm (range 31.3-182.0 cm), and 19/49 (38.8%) were female. 47/49 patients (95.9%) were Caucasian. Median itraconazole daily dose was 5.4 mg/kg/day (range 1.5-12.5) given as Sporanox capsules or oral cyclodextrin solution, with switching between formulations permitted after at least three consecutive doses of the most recent formulation. 75% of capsule doses were taken with food versus 9% of solution doses; food and acidic-beverage effects on Frel were tested as covariates but did not reach statistical significance. 227 itraconazole samples (median 0.264 mg/L) and 192 hydroxy-itraconazole samples (median 0.531 mg/L) were available for the modelling, with the assay LLOQ of 0.075 mg/L imputed at half-LLOQ (0.0375 mg/L) for sub-LLOQ records.

The same metadata is available programmatically via readModelDb("Hennig_2006_itraconazole")$meta$population.

Source trace

The per-parameter origin is recorded as an in-file comment next to each ini() entry in inst/modeldb/specificDrugs/Hennig_2006_itraconazole.R. The table below collects them in one place for review. All parameter values are from Hennig 2006 Table II “Final model” column; structural-model layout and the parent-to-metabolite molecular-weight ratio are taken from the supplied NMTRAN control stream HENNIG S 2006 Clin Pharmacokin Example_Metabolite(Itraconazole)_CTL File.ctl.

Equation / parameter Value Source location
lcl (CL/F itraconazole) log(35.5) -> 35.5 L/h at 70 kg Table II final model
lvc (Vd/F itraconazole) log(672) -> 672 L at 70 kg Table II final model
lcl_ohi (CLm/(F*fm)) log(10.6) -> 10.6 L/h Table II final model
lvc_ohi (Vd(m)/(F*fm)) log(5.29) -> 5.29 L Table II final model
lka (oral solution ka) log(0.96) -> 0.96 h^-1 Table II final model
e_capsule_ka log(0.09 / 0.96) -> -2.367 Table II final model: capsule ka 0.09 h^-1
llag (lag time) log(19.1 / 60) -> 0.318 h Table II final model: 19.1 min
lfdepot (capsule Frel) log(0.55) Table II final model: Frel 0.55
allo_cl (fixed) 0.75 Methods Eq. 5 + .ctl line 20
allo_vc (fixed) 1.0 Methods Eq. 5 + .ctl line 21
etalcl variance log(1 + 0.688^2) = 0.38731 Table II + paper Random Effects Model paragraph
etalvc variance log(1 + 0.758^2) = 0.45404 Table II
cov(etalcl, etalvc) 0.69 * sqrt(var_cl * var_vc) = 0.28938 Random Effects Model paragraph
etalcl_ohi variance log(1 + 0.734^2) = 0.43098 Table II final model: CV 73.4%
etalfdepot variance log(1 + 0.611^2) = 0.31734 Table II final model: CV 61.1%; gated on FORM_CAPSULE
propSd 0.499 (sigma 49.9%) Table II final model
propSd_ohi 0.470 (sigma 47.0%) Table II final model
mw_ratio (722.64 / 705.64) 1.02410 .ctl lines 32 and 35
ODE: d/dt(depot) -ka * depot .ctl $MODEL GUTT + K12 = KA
ODE: d/dt(central) ka * depot - kpm * central .ctl K12 / K20 = 0 / K23 = CL2/V2
ODE: d/dt(central_ohi) kpm * central - kel * central_ohi .ctl K23 / K30 = CL3/V3 / K32 = 0
f(depot) <- exp((lfdepot + etalfdepot) * FORM_CAPSULE) gated F .ctl IF (PREP.EQ.1) F1 = THETA(5) * EXP(ETA(3)) ELSE F1 = 1
lag(depot) <- exp(llag) 0.318 h .ctl ALAG1 = TVLAG / 60

Virtual cohort

Original observed data are not publicly available. The figures below use typical-value (no-IIV) and stochastic VPC-style virtual cohorts whose covariate distributions approximate the paediatric CF/BMT population in Hennig 2006 Table I. Body weights are sampled from a log-normal distribution centred on the median 29.3 kg and bounded at the published 6.8-83.5 kg range; the formulation indicator is split equally between capsule and oral solution (the paper’s analysis pools both) and the dose is fixed at the median 5.4 mg/kg/day.

set.seed(20060501)

mod <- rxode2::rxode(readModelDb("Hennig_2006_itraconazole"))
#> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

dose_mg_per_kg_per_day <- 5.4         # Hennig 2006 Table I median
sim_horizon_h          <- 24          # one once-daily dosing interval

n_per_arm  <- 250

make_cohort <- function(n, capsule, id_offset = 0L) {
  weights <- pmin(pmax(exp(rnorm(n, mean = log(29.3), sd = 0.55)),
                       6.8), 83.5)
  doses_mg <- weights * dose_mg_per_kg_per_day
  base <- tibble(id = id_offset + seq_len(n),
                 WT = weights,
                 FORM_CAPSULE = as.integer(capsule),
                 amt = doses_mg)
  doses <- base |>
    mutate(time = 0, evid = 1L, cmt = "depot")
  obs_grid <- seq(0.1, sim_horizon_h, length.out = 60)
  obs <- base |>
    select(id, WT, FORM_CAPSULE) |>
    tidyr::crossing(time = obs_grid) |>
    mutate(evid = 0L, cmt = "Cc", amt = NA_real_)
  bind_rows(doses, obs) |>
    arrange(id, time, desc(evid)) |>
    mutate(treatment = ifelse(FORM_CAPSULE == 1, "capsule", "oral solution"))
}

events <- bind_rows(
  make_cohort(n_per_arm, capsule = 1, id_offset =          0L),
  make_cohort(n_per_arm, capsule = 0, id_offset = n_per_arm)
)
stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(unique(events[, c("id", "time", "evid")])))

Simulation 

sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(
  object  = mod,
  events  = events,
  keep    = c("WT", "FORM_CAPSULE", "treatment"),
  returnType = "data.frame"
) |>
  filter(time > 0)

For deterministic typical-value replication, zero out the random effects:

sim_typical <- rxode2::rxSolve(
  object  = rxode2::zeroRe(mod),
  events  = events,
  keep    = c("WT", "FORM_CAPSULE", "treatment"),
  returnType = "data.frame"
) |>
  filter(time > 0)
#> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalcl_ohi', 'etalfdepot'
#> Warning: multi-subject simulation without without 'omega'

Replicate published figures

Capsule vs oral solution typical-value profiles (Hennig 2006 Figure 5)

Hennig 2006 Figure 5 panels show the dose-normalised 24-h profile for parent itraconazole and metabolite hydroxy-itraconazole at steady state for the two formulations in a typical 35-kg child receiving 200 mg/day. The figure below runs a single-dose simulation for the same nominal subject (35 kg, 5.4 mg/kg ~ 189 mg) and overlays the capsule and oral-solution arms; the early-Tmax / high-Cmax solution arm and the slower / lower capsule profile reproduce the shape of the published panels.

events_typ <- bind_rows(
  tibble(id = 1L, time = 0, amt = 189, evid = 1L, cmt = "depot",
         WT = 35, FORM_CAPSULE = 1L, treatment = "capsule"),
  tibble(id = 2L, time = 0, amt = 189, evid = 1L, cmt = "depot",
         WT = 35, FORM_CAPSULE = 0L, treatment = "oral solution")
) |>
  bind_rows(
    tidyr::crossing(id = c(1L, 2L), time = seq(0.05, 24, length.out = 240)) |>
      mutate(evid = 0L, cmt = "Cc", amt = NA_real_) |>
      mutate(WT = 35,
             FORM_CAPSULE = ifelse(id == 1L, 1L, 0L),
             treatment = ifelse(id == 1L, "capsule", "oral solution"))
  ) |>
  arrange(id, time, desc(evid))

sim_typ <- rxode2::rxSolve(
  object = rxode2::zeroRe(mod),
  events = events_typ,
  keep   = c("WT", "FORM_CAPSULE", "treatment"),
  returnType = "data.frame"
) |> filter(time > 0)
#> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalcl_ohi', 'etalfdepot'
#> Warning: multi-subject simulation without without 'omega'

sim_typ_long <- sim_typ |>
  pivot_longer(c(Cc, Cc_ohi), names_to = "analyte", values_to = "conc") |>
  mutate(analyte = recode(analyte,
                          Cc     = "Itraconazole",
                          Cc_ohi = "Hydroxy-itraconazole"))

ggplot(sim_typ_long, aes(time, conc, colour = analyte)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ treatment) +
  labs(x = "Time after dose (h)", y = "Plasma concentration (mg/L)",
       colour = NULL,
       title  = "Typical-value 189 mg single-dose profile, 35 kg paediatric subject",
       caption = "Replicates the shape of Hennig 2006 Figure 5 panels (capsule vs oral solution).")

Stochastic VPC-style 5th / 50th / 95th percentiles (Hennig 2006 Figure 5) 

sim_long <- sim |>
  mutate(dose_mg = WT * dose_mg_per_kg_per_day) |>
  pivot_longer(c(Cc, Cc_ohi), names_to = "analyte", values_to = "conc") |>
  mutate(analyte = recode(analyte,
                          Cc     = "Itraconazole",
                          Cc_ohi = "Hydroxy-itraconazole"),
         dnconc  = conc / dose_mg * 200)  # dose-normalised to 200 mg

vpc <- sim_long |>
  group_by(treatment, analyte, time) |>
  summarise(
    Q05 = quantile(dnconc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q50 = quantile(dnconc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q95 = quantile(dnconc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(vpc, aes(time, Q50, colour = analyte, fill = analyte)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.20, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_grid(analyte ~ treatment, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Time after dose (h)",
       y = "Dose-normalised concentration (mg/L per 200 mg)",
       colour = NULL, fill = NULL,
       title = "Stochastic VPC-style profiles (5th, 50th, 95th percentiles)",
       caption = "Reproduces the shape of Hennig 2006 Figure 5 dose-normalised VPC panels.")

PKNCA validation

PKNCA is run separately for the parent (Cc) and the metabolite (Cc_ohi) analytes, each grouped by formulation. The single-dose interval 0-24 h is chosen to match the simulation horizon; aucinf.obs and half.life are computed using PKNCA’s automatic terminal-phase fit.

sim_nca <- sim_typical |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc), time > 0)

dose_df <- events |>
  dplyr::filter(evid == 1) |>
  dplyr::select(id, time, amt, treatment)

conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(
  sim_nca |> dplyr::select(id, time, Cc, treatment),
  Cc ~ time | treatment + id,
  concu = "mg/L", timeu = "hr"
)
dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(dose_df, amt ~ time | treatment + id,
                             doseu = "mg")

intervals_parent <- data.frame(
  start      = 0,
  end        = 24,
  cmax       = TRUE,
  tmax       = TRUE,
  auclast    = TRUE,
  aucinf.obs = TRUE,
  half.life  = TRUE
)

nca_parent <- PKNCA::pk.nca(
  PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals_parent)
)
#> Warning: Requesting an AUC range starting (0) before the first measurement (0.1) is not allowed

nca_parent_summary <- summary(nca_parent)
knitr::kable(nca_parent_summary,
             caption = "Itraconazole (parent) NCA by formulation, typical-value simulation.")
Itraconazole (parent) NCA by formulation, typical-value simulation.
Interval Start Interval End treatment N AUClast (hr*mg/L) Cmax (mg/L) Tmax (hr) Half-life (hr) AUCinf,obs (hr*mg/L)
0 24 capsule 250 NC 0.131 [6.45] 13.1 [11.0, 15.1] 20.9 [3.37] NC
0 24 oral solution 250 NC 0.461 [1.95] 3.34 [2.94, 3.75] 10.7 [1.48] NC 
conc_obj_ohi <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(
  sim_typical |> dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc_ohi), time > 0) |>
    dplyr::select(id, time, Cc_ohi, treatment),
  Cc_ohi ~ time | treatment + id,
  concu = "mg/L", timeu = "hr"
)

intervals_ohi <- data.frame(
  start    = 0,
  end      = 24,
  cmax     = TRUE,
  tmax     = TRUE,
  auclast  = TRUE
)

nca_ohi <- PKNCA::pk.nca(
  PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj_ohi, dose_obj, intervals = intervals_ohi)
)

nca_ohi_summary <- summary(nca_ohi)
knitr::kable(nca_ohi_summary,
             caption = "Hydroxy-itraconazole (metabolite) NCA by formulation, typical-value simulation.")
Hydroxy-itraconazole (metabolite) NCA by formulation, typical-value simulation.
Interval Start Interval End treatment N AUClast (hr*mg/L) Cmax (mg/L) Tmax (hr)
0 24 capsule 250 NC 0.216 [52.3] 13.9 [11.8, 15.5]
0 24 oral solution 250 NC 0.766 [46.1] 4.15 [3.75, 4.15]

Comparison against published values

Hennig 2006 does not publish a per-formulation NCA table to which the simulated NCA can be compared row-for-row, but the paper’s Discussion (page 1106) reports that the final model produced an itraconazole terminal elimination half-life of approximately 10 hours in the paediatric CF/BMT cohort, and the Discussion also notes that the oral solution has an absorption rate ~10x higher than the capsule and ~2x higher relative bioavailability. The simulated NCA above is expected to satisfy:

  • Itraconazole half-life around 10 hours for both formulations in a typical 35-70 kg paediatric subject (volume / clearance gives t1/2 = ln(2) * Vc / CL = 0.693 * 672 / 35.5 = 13.1 h at 70 kg without weight scaling, dropping toward ~10 h once allometric scaling is applied across the median 29.3 kg paediatric weight).
  • Oral-solution Cmax appreciably (~3-4x) higher than capsule Cmax for the same dose, and oral-solution Tmax substantially earlier (~2-4 h) than capsule Tmax (~10-14 h), driven by the formulation-specific ka and the lower capsule relative bioavailability.

Differences between the simulated NCA and these qualitative paper-reported values are not expected to exceed 20% under the typical-value simulation; if they do, investigate the source rather than tuning parameters.

Assumptions and deviations

  • Residual error parameterisation. Hennig 2006 Eq. 3 specifies an exponential residual-error model Cik = Cpred,ik * exp(eps_ik), which the paper also calls “proportional” in the Random Effects Model paragraph. The packaged model uses nlmixr2’s prop(propSd) form (linear-space proportional, Y = F * (1 + propSd * eps)); for the reported residual magnitudes (sigma 49.9% / 47.0%) the two specifications differ by less than about 14% in linear-space CV, well below the 20% validation threshold. A strict log-normal residual-error reproduction would use Cc ~ lnorm(lnSd) with lnSd = sqrt(log(1 + propSd^2)) (~0.471 / 0.443).
  • Fraction metabolised fm fixed to 1. Hennig 2006 Pharmacokinetic Analysis paragraph explicitly fixes fm = 1 because no IV reference data exist to estimate it. As a consequence the apparent metabolite parameters CLm/F and Vd(m)/F are reported on a 1/(F*fm) basis; the absolute metabolite clearance and volume cannot be recovered from the model.
  • Molecular-weight scaling applied to metabolite parameters. The supplied control stream rescales cl_ohi and vc_ohi by 722.64 / 705.64 (the paper-reported ratio of hydroxy-itraconazole to itraconazole molecular weights) inside model() so the predicted central_ohi / vc_ohi is in measured-metabolite mass concentration units (mg/L of OH-itraconazole). The reported Table II values 10.6 L/h and 5.29 L are the THETAs prior to this scaling, matching the .ctl $THETA slots and the published table footnote.
  • No allometric scaling on metabolite parameters. The supplied .ctl applies allometric scaling only to the parent CL2 and V2. The metabolite CL3 and V3 do not carry a body-weight covariate; the packaged model follows this choice (cl_ohi <- exp(lcl_ohi + etalcl_ohi) * mw_ratio with no (WT/70)^... term).
  • Absorption ka via covariate effect on a canonical lka. The paper estimates two separate THETAs for ka (capsule 0.09 h^-1 and oral solution 0.96 h^-1). The packaged model encodes this with one canonical lka (the oral-solution value) plus a binary e_capsule_ka covariate effect that is added to lka when FORM_CAPSULE == 1. This preserves the canonical lka parameter name and standard e_<cov>_<param> covariate-effect form while reproducing the exact two-formulation behaviour.
  • Relative-bioavailability IIV gated on the capsule arm only. The supplied .ctl applies the EXP(ETA(3)) BSV term on F1 only when PREP.EQ.1. The packaged model achieves the same behaviour with f(depot) <- exp((lfdepot + etalfdepot) * FORM_CAPSULE): when FORM_CAPSULE == 0 the exponent collapses to 0 and Frel = 1 exactly; when FORM_CAPSULE == 1 the exponent is lfdepot + etalfdepot and the IIV etalfdepot is realised.
  • Lag time identical for capsule and oral solution. The .ctl uses a single ALAG1 value for both formulations (no formulation-specific switching on the lag), and the paper’s Table II reports a single 19.1 min lag time. The packaged model follows this single-llag choice.
  • Simulation virtual cohort. Body weights are sampled from a log-normal distribution centred on the published median (29.3 kg) and clipped to the published range (6.8-83.5 kg); 95.9% of the source cohort was Caucasian and race / disease state are not in the final-model covariate equations, so the vignette does not stratify on race or disease.
  • Errata. No erratum / corrigendum to Hennig 2006 was located on disk for this extraction; the operator should reconfirm against the Adis / Springer corrections feed for Clin Pharmacokinet 45(11) before relying on the model in production.

On this page
